elements.sent-2411-001: The entanglement asymmetry measures the extent to which a symmetry is broken within a subsystem of an extended quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-002: Here, we analyse this quantity in Haar random states for arbitrary compact, semi-simple Lie groups, building on and generalising recent results obtained for the $U(1)$ symmetric case.
elements.sent-2411-003: We find that, for any symmetry group, the average entanglement asymmetry vanishes in the thermodynamic limit when the subsystem is smaller than its complement.
elements.sent-2411-004: When the subsystem and its complement are of equal size, the entanglement asymmetry jumps to a finite value, indicating a sudden transition of the subsystem from a fully symmetric state to one devoid of any symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-005: For larger subsystem sizes, the entanglement asymmetry displays a logarithmic scaling with a coefficient fixed by the dimension of the group.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also investigate the fluctuations of the entanglement asymmetry, which tend to zero in the thermodynamic limit.
elements.sent-2411-007: We check our findings against exact numerical calculations for the $SU(2)$ and $SU(3)$ groups.
elements.sent-2411-008: We further discuss their implications for the thermalisation of isolated quantum systems and black hole evaporation.
elements.sent-2411-009: Their essential idea was to describe overly complicated systems, for which an exact solution is unfeasible, as a black box in which a very large number of degrees of freedom interact according to unknown laws.
elements.sent-2411-010: Then, from a minimum set of assumptions and relying on typicality arguments, some generic, universal properties of the spectrum are predicted by a specific ensemble of random Hamiltonians conveniently chosen.
elements.sent-2411-011: Random matrices have been successfully applied to many other areas of physics, backed by a robust mathematical framework and a well-established set of methods that enable analytic calculations~\cite{m-04, f-10 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, we can mention the characterisation of quantum chaos and the thermalisation of isolated quantum systems~\cite{bgs-84, d-91, s-94, akpr-16, klp-18 , transport in disordered media~\cite{b-97 , or the encoding of quantum information~\cite{cn-16 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the same spirit as Wigner and Dyson, a natural approach to understanding the properties of generic many-body quantum states is to exploit random matrix theory.
elements.sent-2411-014: This question has recently garnered significant attention.
elements.sent-2411-015: The common wisdom is that random quantum states, constructed using random matrices drawn from a particular ensemble, describe the qualitative behaviour of the excited eigenstates of typical Hamiltonians~\cite{vr-17, nwfs-18, fnsw-18, lg-19, bhkrv-22 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: They are also expected to capture the universal features of states resulting from sufficiently chaotic dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this regard, the works~\cite{p-93, p-93-1 by Page on the black hole information paradox represent a turning point.}
elements.sent-2411-018: By assuming that black holes follow a highly chaotic unitary dynamics, Page proposed to effectively describe them and the radiation emitted during their evaporation as a system of qubits in a random state.
elements.sent-2411-019: The central result of Page's papers is that, for a quantum system with an infinite number of degrees of freedom and divided into two parts $A$ and $B$, the average entanglement entropy over pure states distributed according to the Haar measure is the maximal one, \begin{equation}\label{eq:page_curve} \mathbb{E}[S_1(\rho_A)]\simeq \min(\log d_A, \log d_B),\end{equation} where $d_A$ and $d_B$ are the dimensions of the Hilbert spaces of $A$ and $B$ respectively and $S_1(\rho)=-{\rm Tr [\rho\log\rho]$ is the von Neumann entropy of the $A$'reduced density matrix $\rho_A$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Eq.~\eqref{eq:page_curve is usually known as Page curve and has been proven in different ways~\cite{fk-94, s-95, s-96 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: It implies that, for very large systems, typical quantum states have maximal bipartite entanglement.
elements.sent-2411-022: In black hole theory, the results obtained by Page are today a fundamental guiding principle in solving the information paradox~\cite{h-75, h-76 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, through the replica wormhole approach, several recent works~\cite{aemm-19, penington-20, ahmst-20, pssy-22 have found in certain specific quantum gravity models that the entanglement entropy of the radiation satisfies the Page curve and information is not lost during the evaporation.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Eq.~\eqref{eq:page_curve also plays an essential role in understanding the thermalisation of isolated quantum systems following a chaotic evolution with no conservation laws, since the ensemble of Haar random states describes their late-time behaviour~\cite{akpr-16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The work of Page also paved the way for investigating other entanglement properties of generic many-body quantum systems, usually a very challenging task, by applying random matrix theory.
elements.sent-2411-026: For Haar random states, there are many results on their higher entanglement moments~\cite{csz-06,g-07,vpo-16,w-17 , entanglement spectrum~\cite{z-06,mbl-08,dffp-13 , mixed-state purity~\cite{depasqualeetal , negativity~\cite{slkv-21 , or relative entropy~\cite{kf-21 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Other ensembles of random states have been also considered.
elements.sent-2411-028: For example, the typical behaviour of non-interacting systems is described by random Gaussian states~\cite{vhbr-17, vhbr-18, hvrb-19, lrv-20, lrv-21 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The analogue of the Page curve for the latter has been obtained in Refs.~\cite{lcb-18, zlc-20, bhk-21, bp-21 .}

elements.sent-2411-031: A very interesting aspect of random states is the analysis of their symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-032: One possibility is to consider an ensemble of random states with a global internal symmetry and investigate its effects on the properties of the ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-033: For example, one can examine the symmetry resolution of entanglement~\cite{lr-14,gs-18,xas-18,cdm-21 , that is how entanglement distributes among the symmetry sectors, as has been done in random states both for Abelian~\cite{bd-19,lntt-22,mcp-22,g-24 and non-Abelian symmetry groups~\cite{bdk-24 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Equally relevant is the analysis of the broken symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-035: A crucial point, which has been largely overlooked until very recently, is that, in many-body quantum systems, the breaking of a symmetry inherently depends on the particular subsystem that we consider, due to the presence of long-range correlations that do not respect the symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, a natural candidate to diagnose symmetry breaking is a quantity based on entanglement measures.
elements.sent-2411-037: This is the idea behind the entanglement asymmetry, which measures the degree to which a symmetry is broken in a part of the system in terms of the entanglement entropy~\cite{amc-23 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Using this observable, the breaking of an arbitrary $U(1)$ symmetry in a system of qubits in Haar random states, identical to the one considered by Page, has been recently analysed in Ref.~\cite{ampc-24 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The main result of that work is the following.
elements.sent-2411-040: If we take an infinite system and we divide it into two parts $A$ and $B$, then the state of $A$ is $U(1)$ symmetric for $d_A<d_B$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Surprisingly, at $d_A=d_B$, the subsystem $A$ undergoes a sharp transition to a state that breaks the symmetry when $d_A>d_B$.
elements.sent-2411-042: As discussed in Ref.~\cite{ampc-24 , this result might have important consequences for the symmetries of the radiation emitted in the evaporation of a black hole, as well as for the symmetries of the local stationary state to which a generic isolated non-equilibrium system with no conserved charges relaxes.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: The explicit example of the $SU(N)$ group.
elements.sent-2411-294: As an example, we specialise the result in Eq.~\eqref{typvNEA_G to the semi-simple compact Lie Group $SU(N)$.}

elements.sent-2411-323: To this end, we employed the entanglement asymmetry, an entanglement-based observable that quantifies how much a symmetry is broken in a part of an extended quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-324: Our findings generalise the recent results presented in Ref.~\cite{ampc-24 for the $U(1)$ group, extending them to encompass any compact, semi-simple Lie symmetry group.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In the large system-size $L$ limit, the average entanglement asymmetry is zero when the subsystem size $\ell_A$ satisfies $\ell_A<L/2$.
elements.sent-2411-326: At $\ell_A=L/2$, it suddenly jumps to a finite value, meaning that there is a sharp transition in the subsystem $A$ from a state that respects any symmetry to a non-symmetric state.
elements.sent-2411-327: The vanishing of the entanglement asymmetry for any symmetry group when $\ell_A < L/2$ can be understood through the Hayden-Preskill decoupling inequality~\eqref{dec_ineq .}
elements.sent-2411-328: This inequality states that, for $\ell_A < L/2$, the reduced density matrix of subsystem A is, on average, exponentially close to the (normalised) identity matrix, which is inherently symmetric under any group.
elements.sent-2411-329: However, the decoupling inequality does not explain the behaviour of the entanglement asymmetry when $\ell_A\geq L/2$, particularly the abrupt discontinuity observed at $\ell_A=L/2$.
elements.sent-2411-330: For $\ell_A>L/2$, the entanglement asymmetry grows logarithmically in $\ell_A$ with a coefficient fixed by the dimension of the group.
elements.sent-2411-331: In this regime, we also calculated the exact value of the $\ell_A$-independent term for any value of the R\'enyi index, which is expressed in terms of a few specific parameters of the symmetry group.
elements.sent-2411-332: As an illustration, we explicitly computed it for the $SU(N)$ group.
elements.sent-2411-333: We also discussed the size of the fluctuations of the asymmetry around the average value.
elements.sent-2411-334: We showed that they go to zero as the system size $L$ increases and, therefore, the average entanglement asymmetry that we computed is also the typical one.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our results have relevance in multiple contexts, as the states we are considering can be thought as representatives of excited eigenstates of chaotic many-body Hamiltonians.
elements.sent-2411-336: As we already mentioned, our system of random qudits is similar to the one originally studied by Page in~\cite{p-93, p-93-1 to model the evaporation of a black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-337: In that case, we can think that the subsystem $A$ is the radiation emitted while $B$ is the black hole.
elements.sent-2411-338: Before it starts to evaporate, all the qudits are in $B$ and, therefore, $\ell_A=0$.
elements.sent-2411-339: As the black hole evaporates, the qudits are transferred to $A$, mimicking the emission of radiation, until $\ell_A=L$ when the black hole is fully evaporated.
elements.sent-2411-340: In this process, we can identify time as $t\propto \ell_A$.
elements.sent-2411-341: As already discussed in Ref.~\cite{ampc-24 , our results imply that, if the black hole is initially in a state that breaks any symmetry and information is preserved during its evaporation, then the radiation emitted is in a fully symmetric state until the halfway point of the process, $\ell_A=L/2$, i.e. the Page time.}
elements.sent-2411-342: At that moment, not only does the entanglement entropy of the radiation begin to decrease, following the Page curve~\eqref{eq:page_curve , but the state also abruptly loses any symmetry, mirroring the symmetry breaking that initially occurred in the black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-343: Thus, until the Page time, studying the radiation alone does not reveal whether the black hole breaks any symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-344: These conclusions are derived under fairly general assumptions and, much like the arguments of Wigner and Dyson for nuclei, are likely to hold in specific models of quantum gravity based on principles of typicality.
elements.sent-2411-345: For example, the sudden transition in the radiation from a symmetric to a non-symmetric state has been also observed in a particular theory of quantum gravity applying the replica wormhole formalism~\cite{cl-21 .}
